words: SAM SCHRAGER: The first thing I wanted to ask you was how your
interest in local history came about, whether it started
when you were a boy or when you were looking back on it?
JOHN B. MILLER: Well, I think it did start when I was a boy.
I think I really started to be aware of it back in 1928.
We were back in Wisconsin in '28 and '29, we spent a winter
back there, and we used to get this Bovill high school paper
here. And they published it, oh, probably about a two page
resume of the history of Bovill that they'd gotten from
T. P. Jones. And my dad was going through that and pick
ing out things that he thought were wrong in it, as all oldtimers
do. Every time anybody mentions a history or pub
lishes a history, why the oldtimers all always start to
pick it to pieces and then they pick. He started to do that,
plus embellish it with a lot of other things that he said
ought to have been mentioned. And, of course, I'd always
heard him talking ajfeut the pioneer days and the early things
and so forth, and had quite a background in it. And I think
at the time I began to get aware of it; plus the fact that I
was always very much interested in old photographs, they
always intrigued me. I guess it grew out of that, I don't
know that.

speaker: SAM
words: When were you born and how old were you then?

speaker: JBM
words: Well, I was born in 1912. In '28 see I was about sixteen

speaker: JBM
words: at that time.

speaker: SAM
words: And your folks had moved from.

speaker: JBM
words: They'd moved from Bovill. Well, we'd gone east, we'd

speaker: JBM
words: just gone east to visit some of his folks, and when we got

speaker: JBM
words: there, why his brother talked us into staying over a winter.

speaker: JBM
words: He wanted us to spend the winter back there. My folks

speaker: JBM
words: decided to stay; they rented a house. Among us we picked

speaker: JBM
words: up work to support ourselves over winter, and spent the

speaker: JBM
words: winter there. Moved beck the next year. That's when we

speaker: JBM
words: moved from the place there just south of town here. (We

speaker: JBM
words: already owned the meadow, the Little Meadow out here south

speaker: JBM
words: east of town, and we also owned this forty-seven acres south

speaker: JBM
words: of the treating plant. And we had a house on that but we'd

speaker: JBM
words: rented that out, so when we came back we moved onto Little

speaker: JBM
words: Meadow out southeast of town here, and built a house there.)

speaker: SAM
words: Do you feel that it just continued to grow, your interest

speaker: SAM
words: in history, as you grew up?

speaker: JBM
words: Oh, yes, I think it did. I suppose I was always interest

speaker: JBM
words: ed in history. History was interesting to me as a small child,

speaker: JBM
words: but a small child doesn't quite realize he's living among

speaker: JBM
words: history, and that his own small community has a history too.

speaker: JBM
words: This doesn't quite come to you until you suddenly discover

speaker: JBM
words: that things are getting a long ways behind, and that you know

speaker: JBM
words: thiags that or heard about things that other people or younger

speaker: JBM
words: people haven't experienced and known, and it suddenly comes

speaker: JBM
words: through. I don't really believe that pioneers in general are

speaker: JBM
words: very much aware of history, they're so tied up in the present.

speaker: JBM
words: When a community begins to develop a little past, why, people

speaker: JBM
words: are going to start to get fascinated with it, and that's the

speaker: JBM
words: trend I see in the present day people. That's why I think

speaker: JBM
words: people are so interested when somebody does sit down and

speaker: JBM
words: write up some thing that is readable.

speaker: SAM
words: Could you tell how you went about collecting the material

speaker: SAM
words: that made up pie Trees Grew Tall?

speaker: JBM
words: Haphazardly (laugh). I had no experience in it at all.

speaker: JBM
words: I suppose if I didn't have a framework, a thread of a story,

speaker: JBM
words: of the sequence of things in mind, it would've been a terrific

speaker: JBM
words: task. But I did have a framework, and that gives you some

speaker: JBM
words: thing to hang something on. And it also sort of serves as

speaker: JBM
words: a filter for, you know, when you hear something, if it fits

speaker: JBM
words: in... Well of course if it's true, it always fits in, it has

speaker: JBM
words: to fit in. But when you start talking to people you hear of

speaker: JBM
words: so many things that you were just unaware of completely, and

speaker: JBM
words: it fills that framework, begins to fill that framework in

speaker: JBM
words: pretty fast.

speaker: JBM
words: ROB MOORE: Did you decide to write a book and then start gather

speaker: JBM
words: ing material? Or had you been gathering material first?

speaker: JBM
words: I would have to say (I think I say a little about that

speaker: JBM
words: in the introduction of the book here), I had started gather

speaker: JBM
words: ing material, but perhaps not actually noting things down in

speaker: JBM
words: note form and things like that. But I had put several news

speaker: JBM
words: paper items aside that had covered history, and I had been

speaker: JBM
words: already grabbing pictures whenever I had a chance. Whenever

speaker: JBM
words: there was a chance for me to get ahold of a picture that

speaker: JBM
words: was of historical interest, I had put ittfway, either a

speaker: JBM
words: clipping out of a newspaper, or in some instances an actual

speaker: JBM
words: photograph. To this extent I had gathered some material.

speaker: JBM
words: When I started working on this book, I had no intention

speaker: JBM
words: really... My original plan had never really been to write

speaker: JBM
words: a book, but do something in a much smaller scale, of the type

speaker: JBM
words: of thing that you people are interested in, you know—just

speaker: JBM
words: getting the information and recording it and putting away

speaker: JBM
words: someplace where somebody else could get ahold of it, before

speaker: JBM
words: it was lost. It just seemed to me such a shame that all this

speaker: JBM
words: information was being lost, because it was in people's mem

speaker: JBM
words: ories and nobody was getting it down.

speaker: SAM
words: Are there certain people you depended on in particular

speaker: SAM
words: for information, and if there were, why were they such good

speaker: SAM
words: informants for you?

speaker: JBM
words: Only to the extent that I knew who the old people were

speaker: JBM
words: around town that were associated with the town in the early

speaker: JBM
words: days. I knew them all. They were the natural people to go

speaker: JBM
words: to. My facilities to reach people out of Bovill were rather

speaker: JBM
words: limited because it takes time, you have to go a hundred miles

speaker: JBM
words: to talk to somebody, and you can probably talk to three or

speaker: JBM
words: four people locally while you're doing that. Since I had

speaker: JBM
words: a pretty limited time budget I had to think of that a great

speaker: JBM
words: deal. I did go to a few of those people and I wrote letters

speaker: JBM
words: to others. And I sent inquires to a few through friends, to

speaker: JBM
words: settle certain issues. But it was pretty apparent, after I

speaker: JBM
words: had done a couple of stints up in the Bovill area interview

speaker: JBM
words: ing people, that I pretty much had enough material, so far

speaker: JBM
words: as volume was concerned, to do me. The main thing was to

speaker: JBM
words: verify as much as I could, and settle on dates. There were

speaker: JBM
words: so many discrepencies.

speaker: SAM
words: How did you go about resolving the conflicts?

speaker: JBM
words: As much as I could, I finally had to go into the Uni

speaker: JBM
words: versity library and dig out newspapers and things like that.

speaker: JBM
words: Well, Gwen Lawrence's (the former Gwen Bovill's) diary. It

speaker: JBM
words: was her mother's diary that she had used, and she had compiled

speaker: JBM
words: this into a manuscript for a book. I had access to that.

speaker: JBM
words: That settled a great many things. And then I went into

speaker: JBM
words: the library stacks down at the university to scrounge up

speaker: JBM
words: more data. In the odds and ends of course there are a lot

speaker: JBM
words: of things there was just no way to really resolve. Very

speaker: JBM
words: often on those things, I reported them in such a way that

speaker: JBM
words: my wording is not that specific about small details. I

speaker: JBM
words: didn't give dates sometimes, or when I told something as

speaker: JBM
words: a story, I told it as a story rather than as necessarily

speaker: JBM
words: an absolute fact. Or I mention a question, that some date

speaker: JBM
words: is questionable at times.

speaker: JBM
words: Nevertheless I run into challanges all the time on

speaker: JBM
words: the veracity of what I've said, and I feel in many instances

speaker: JBM
words: it comes to this nitpicking type of criticism that I've

speaker: JBM
words: mentioned. It often hinges on the fact that one person

speaker: JBM
words: remembers things differently than another, and I'm veryjwell

speaker: JBM
words: aware that the day after something happens you can ask two

speaker: JBM
words: people a question as to what happened, and you don't get

speaker: JBM
words: the same story. After fifty or sixty years it's kind of

speaker: JBM
words: easy to understand why it might happen.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you think that's the most difficult thing about trying

speaker: SAM
words: to capture the past of a small place?

speaker: JBM
words: It's very difficult to interview people and actually

speaker: JBM
words: come up with precisely what happened at any time or precise

speaker: JBM
words: thing about the order of events, and things like this. This

speaker: JBM
words: is what they're very often confused about, is the order

speaker: JBM
words: which happened. While I was interviewing on these railroads

speaker: JBM
words: for example, I had a spread of about three years there that

speaker: JBM
words: nobody seemed to be sure what happened, or what year the...

speaker: JBM
words: They couldn't agree on the year that the railroads were

speaker: JBM
words: completed, for example. I knew pretty well, but still, you

speaker: JBM
words: begin to question your own information when you hear oldtimers

speaker: JBM
words: say, "Well no that wasn't so, something else was so."

speaker: JBM
words: And until I could get papers that actually showed, to prove

speaker: JBM
words: the dates of completion, why, you have to wonder if you might

speaker: JBM
words: not be wrong about something.

speaker: JBM
words: For example, that first meeting we held over here at

speaker: JBM
words: the church, they argued around about this, and so-and-so

speaker: JBM
words: says, "Well, the excursion train came in here in 1910."

speaker: JBM
words: "Well no it didn't, I rode it in 1907," and that sort of

speaker: JBM
words: thing. When you start going back into history and inquiring

speaker: JBM
words: what happened, there were two excursion trains, one in each

speaker: JBM
words: year--one of them commemerating the completion of the W.I.M.

speaker: JBM
words: railroad thru and the second one commemorating the junction

speaker: JBM
words: of the two railroads when the Milwaukie completed their line.

speaker: JBM
words: And both stories are true.

speaker: ROB
words: You said you already had established a filter, some idea

speaker: ROB
words: yourself of what had happened. How much did this idea have

speaker: ROB
words: to change through that sort of...

speaker: JBM
words: Oh, there had to be changes. There were things that

speaker: JBM
words: I had in my mind that were probably basically correct in

speaker: JBM
words: context, but were shifted off in time, or were a little

speaker: JBM
words: wrong in some part of the details. As I would have told the

speaker: JBM
words: details they wouldn't have been wrong, I mean, from my own

speaker: JBM
words: recollection. They wouldn't have been out of context with

speaker: JBM
words: what happened, but they would not have been entirely factual.

speaker: JBM
words: And these are the things you have to check on, and get as

speaker: JBM
words: nearly factual as you can. But, as I say, it does form a

speaker: JBM
words: framework to fill in, and you're surprised at how fast it

speaker: JBM
words: does fill in, when you start interviews. But the time is

speaker: JBM
words: taken, then, when you run into discrepancies.

speaker: JBM
words: And it's also taken filling in. I found that I was

speaker: JBM
words: quite unskilled at interviewing people and the first inter

speaker: JBM
words: view getting all the detail I wanted. They'd go over the

speaker: JBM
words: information, and, for example) I didn't have a tape record

speaker: JBM
words: er, and I'd try to sit there and take notes. And I'd end

speaker: JBM
words: up the notes, and I'd go back and start to write something

speaker: JBM
words: about it, and I'd find I'd missed two or three names. Well,

speaker: JBM
words: they mentioned names there, see, and I don't have those names,

speaker: JBM
words: and so I'd have to backtrack to people and ask them again.

speaker: JBM
words: I had that sort of thin happening all the time.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you have thoughts, from your study and your own exper

speaker: SAM
words: ience here, about the character of the Bovill area, about

speaker: SAM
words: what's unique to it?

speaker: JBM
words: Well, I don't really know that there's anything so

speaker: JBM
words: unique about it. Among these communities back in this part

speaker: JBM
words: of Latah County, of course, it's tucked back in the woods

speaker: JBM
words: farther, and depended almost entirely on timber, rather than

speaker: JBM
words: on farming, which, as you go westward, the county becomes

speaker: JBM
words: more and more dependent on farming and less and less on its

speaker: JBM
words: trees. Then the Bovill community of course depended just

speaker: JBM
words: almost totally on the forest as a source of occupation.

speaker: JBM
words: Another thing, as compared with towns such as St. Maries and

speaker: JBM
words: other towns to the north that are fundamentally logging communities,

speaker: JBM
words: of course, Bovill was a Potlatch Company town.

speaker: JBM
words: Not owned by the Potlatch Company, I don't mean that, but

speaker: JBM
words: it was established because of the forests of the Potlatch

speaker: JBM
words: Company, rather than other logging interests. It was a

speaker: JBM
words: juncture point, of course, for the railroads, and that was

speaker: JBM
words: very important to the town. And of course Elk River then

speaker: JBM
words: was a mill town, Potlatch was a mill town, and Bovill was

speaker: JBM
words: primarily a town that was set up to move logs.

speaker: JBM
words: And it was really in the prime part of the white pine,

speaker: JBM
words: I think the best white pine in almost all North Idaho was

speaker: JBM
words: right around Bovill here. There was a lot of white pine in

speaker: JBM
words: North Idaho but there was none of it that equalled that

speaker: JBM
words: stands that were around Bovill. So those are certain things

speaker: JBM
words: that characterized this town. The fact's that more logging

speaker: JBM
words: crews moved out of Bovill than any other town. Even much

speaker: JBM
words: of the operations around Elk River and Deary (had) Bovill

speaker: JBM
words: men involved, much more than there were Elk River and Deary

speaker: JBM
words: people involved at Bovill, because this is just a kind of

speaker: JBM
words: a natural center for all of this logging area.

speaker: SAM
words: Could you draw differences between the way people were in

speaker: SAM
words: Bovill as compared to a farming town like Kendrick or Genesee,

speaker: SAM
words: that's just besides the fact that here they log and there

speaker: SAM
words: they farm? Does it make for a different kind of society?

speaker: JBM
words: I'm sure that the logger fundamentally has a different

speaker: JBM
words: attitude than the farmer. Anytime that you go into the family

speaker: JBM
words: people, the old time family people were conservative people

speaker: JBM
words: in general, but the farmmt by nature is much more conserative

speaker: JBM
words: and careful. He has to become much more businesslike in his

speaker: JBM
words: attitude or he would go broke. He has to run a closer

speaker: JBM
words: business, whereas a logger up here of course is working

speaker: JBM
words: for a monthly wage. He's got a steady income, and he knows

speaker: JBM
words: just about what it will do, and he lives in this way. He

speaker: JBM
words: is not forced, as a farmer is, to maybe work fourteen,

speaker: JBM
words: fifteen hours. He's on a regular work schedule, and he has

speaker: JBM
words: more free time at night. The logger very often, if he was

speaker: JBM
words: engaged in a town, and worked right there in the community,

speaker: JBM
words: lived at home. But many of these people, of course, spent

speaker: JBM
words: their time, their nights, in a lumber camp, excepting perhaps

speaker: JBM
words: weekends with the family—those who worked out in the camps.

speaker: JBM
words: This is quite a different life than the farmer has.

speaker: ROB
words: Loggers, especially the oldtime lumberjacks, have a really

speaker: ROB
words: strong reputation for their so many tall tales, they're pic

speaker: ROB
words: tured as being hell raisers, and that sort of image surrounds

speaker: ROB
words: them. Do you think that was a fairly accurate representation?

speaker: JBM
words: I think it probably overstressed. Mrs. Wandke down

speaker: JBM
words: here was showing me a book that came out, what community is

speaker: JBM
words: it? It's one of the North Idaho towns, anyway, it deals

speaker: JBM
words: with, it's something about caulked boots or something, I

speaker: JBM
words: was glancing through it.

speaker: SAM
words: Bert Russell.

speaker: JBM
words: Russell, that's right. I got the impression from it

speaker: JBM
words: that it tended... I din't read the book. It's an unfair

speaker: JBM
words: criticism, I guess, but just looking at tne chapter headings

speaker: JBM
words: and the way the book was set up, I sort of got the impression

speaker: JBM
words: that it might be written a bit for the sensational side, you

speaker: JBM
words: know, and may tend to overcharacterize the lumberjack as a

speaker: JBM
words: ruffian. The lumberjack was a hard drinking man, there's

speaker: JBM
words: no doubt of it. They used lots of liquor, and they lived

speaker: JBM
words: back in the lurabercamps, you know, until they were really

speaker: JBM
words: bent to get out and let off steam, and they'd head for Spo

speaker: JBM
words: kane or something and do it. But still, they weren't really

speaker: JBM
words: that ruffians as storybooks are inclined to picture them.

speaker: JBM
words: Anytime that you engage people in a hard and dangerous phy

speaker: JBM
words: sical work, you're sure to have people who have a ruffian's

speaker: JBM
words: point of view, I guess, about things. As I say, I think it

speaker: JBM
words: was that part has been overstressed in books, probably.

speaker: JBM
words: They were pretty plain people in general. They were tran

speaker: JBM
words: sients to a large extent. They were largely bachelors, ex

speaker: JBM
words: cepting for these key men that lived in areas like Bovill,

speaker: JBM
words: who were family men. But the typical lumberjack in the

speaker: JBM
words: lumbercamp was a transient bachelor, I would say probably

speaker: JBM
words: three-quarters of the crew.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you have much contact, in this thing, with what with

speaker: ROB
words: what lumberjacks thought of themselves? I know the whole

speaker: ROB
words: thing about an outsider looking at lumberjack has his image

speaker: ROB
words: of a rough-and-ready sort of, you know... What did the lum

speaker: ROB
words: berjack think of his own job, his own self, his own relat

speaker: ROB
words: ionship to the company, things like that?

speaker: JBM
words: Probably the man that ought t answer a question like

speaker: JBM
words: that would be somebody like Homer Pelton, who was one and

speaker: JBM
words: who worked closer to them, or a man like Jerry Carlin, who

speaker: JBM
words: lives down the street, because they knew these people a lot

speaker: JBM
words: better than I do. I have the impression that the lumberjack

speaker: JBM
words: like almost every class of people, they have a pretty good

speaker: JBM
words: opinion of their own image. If they are rough-and-ready

speaker: JBM
words: people, it's because they admire people who are rough-andready,

speaker: JBM
words: and like to fit into the image. I didn't know of

speaker: JBM
words: very many fights among lumberjacks, for example. They're

speaker: JBM
words: pretty amiable people. They get along well together. They

speaker: JBM
words: like each other. Each man appreciates that the other man

speaker: JBM
words: is a hard worker, that he's tough, and he can get out, and

speaker: JBM
words: take it and do a man's share. And he can swear as good

speaker: JBM
words: as anything and he can drink as hard as any, and so forth.

speaker: JBM
words: And these are sort of the images that they aspire to, and

speaker: JBM
words: they try to fit the pattern as well as they can. But I

speaker: JBM
words: don't really think they enjoy brawling and that sort of

speaker: JBM
words: thing any more that anybody else does, you know. Occassionally

speaker: JBM
words: somebody gets drunk and probably gets in a fight. But

speaker: JBM
words: no man is going to go around with a chip in his shoulder,

speaker: JBM
words: and be spoiling for a fight all the time, and be very pop

speaker: JBM
words: ular in any crew. And when you talk about somebody roughand-

speaker: JBM
words: ready, as much as anything I think you're probably

speaker: JBM
words: talking about his ability to get out and do a hard day's

speaker: JBM
words: work and not complain, and take all kinds of rough knocks

speaker: JBM
words: on the job, and get along well with the people around him,

speaker: JBM
words: and behave pretty much like they do. And I think this is

speaker: JBM
words: really what you're... The life is a rough and ready life,

speaker: JBM
words: that's kind of what it boils down to. Now someplace in

speaker: JBM
words: this book I have this picture of this man they call the

speaker: JBM
words: Lumberjack Preacher.

speaker: SAM
words: Rights Dick Farrell

speaker: JBM
words: Dick Farrell. I suspect that one of the reasons he

speaker: JBM
words: was admired so much is that he had been a pugilist. The

speaker: JBM
words: lumberjacks followed the—an awful lot of them, they fol

speaker: JBM
words: lowed the professional prizefighters. They admired them.

speaker: JBM
words: They were physical men, and the lumberjacks appreciate a

speaker: JBM
words: physical man. And here was a guy that had been a prize

speaker: JBM
words: fighter, and he'd turned preacher, and probably much more

speaker: JBM
words: interesting to 'em because he was a prizefighter, and not

speaker: JBM
words: only that, he was a good rough and tumble fighter. They

speaker: JBM
words: knew that here was a preacher that come along and lick any

speaker: JBM
words: man in camp, probably.

speaker: JBM
words: My brother-in-law was kind of the same way, you know—

speaker: JBM
words: Bill Musch. He had been a prizefighter in his younger days«

speaker: JBM
words: I don't think there's, in the average lumbercamp that he

speaker: JBM
words: worked, I don't think there'd be a man on the crew that

speaker: JBM
words: could lick him. But I never knew him to fight anybody.

speaker: JBM
words: When he went to work for the CCC camps out here, he gave

speaker: JBM
words: boxing instructions to the young kids. He was a man then,

speaker: JBM
words: I guess, of close ot fifty years old. He had the fastest

speaker: JBM
words: hands I ever he could move those hands. He'd

speaker: JBM
words: take—he'd pour these hotcakes out on the griddle like that,

speaker: JBM
words: and he'd go along with a flipper, and just a series of move

speaker: JBM
words: ments up and down with his hands, and as fast as that hand d

speaker: JBM
words: move up and down, why that hotcake turner'd get under the

speaker: JBM
words: edge of a hotcake and flip it over. He'd flip over sixty

speaker: JBM
words: hotcakes in about sixty seconds. Precision hands.

speaker: JBM
words: And when he worked for the CCC out here, he gave these

speaker: JBM
words: boxing instructions; and we used to pick up these fellows

speaker: JBM
words: and haul them out to camp once in a while, when we'd be

speaker: JBM
words: driving out that way. I remember my dad talking to one of

speaker: JBM
words: the boys. He says the boy was telling him Billy Musch was

speaker: JBM
words: showing them how. They had boxing teams on the CCC camps,

speaker: JBM
words: and he said, "Billy Musch is our boxing coach." And dad

speaker: JBM
words: says, he says "Yen, he used to be a pretty good boxer when

speaker: JBM
words: he was young, " and the kid say "Yah, there isn't anybody

speaker: JBM
words: in camp he can't slap now if he wants to." But of course

speaker: JBM
words: he had been a pro boxer, and he'd get those hands through

speaker: JBM
words: there and you just couldn't stop 'em. A person who wasn't

speaker: JBM
words: of that category... Sure, he didn't have the stamina to

speaker: JBM
words: fight any more or anything like that, but those hands were

speaker: JBM
words: just as vicious as they'd ever been. He'd sneak them

speaker: JBM
words: through.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you think that pretty definite lines were drawn between

speaker: SAM
words: the guys who lived in the camps and were transient loggers,

speaker: SAM
words: and the people who were loggers with families here? Was

speaker: SAM
words: there much of a distinction in Bovill life?

speaker: JBM
words: Well of course the loggers were just back and forth

speaker: JBM
words: through here, mainly, I mean the transients were back and

speaker: JBM
words: forth through. They often had good friends in town, usually

speaker: JBM
words: did. An awful lot of them at least did have good friends

speaker: JBM
words: in town that they knew. They knew the local people and the

speaker: JBM
words: local people knew them, by and large. There was a tendency

speaker: JBM
words: for the same crew to come back to the same area. There'd

speaker: JBM
words: be some filtering back and shifting forth, back and forth

speaker: JBM
words: between lumber camps, you know, but an awful lot of the log

speaker: JBM
words: gers get to liking an area, and they like a certain boss,

speaker: JBM
words: and they go back there. So even though they are in and out..

speaker: JBM
words: They'll quit a job and go up to Spokane and blow off their

speaker: JBM
words: steam, and spend their money, and very often they came back

speaker: JBM
words: into the same area to work.

speaker: ROB
words: Would a lot of the transient men stick around, like when

speaker: ROB
words: the camps were closed during the mud season or during the

speaker: ROB
words: bad season, when they weren't working? Would they stick

speaker: ROB
words: around, or would they go off...?

speaker: JBM
words: Some of them did. There were places, for example, the

speaker: JBM
words: boarding houses and hotels here. The big ones of course

speaker: JBM
words: were the Bovill Hotel and the Spokane Hotel and the David

speaker: JBM
words: Hotel. I have the impression—I don't know whether this

speaker: JBM
words: is quite true all the way along the line, but I think that,

speaker: JBM
words: by and large, the David Hotel and the Bovill Hotel catered

speaker: JBM
words: more to rather long-term boarders and people, you know, who

speaker: JBM
words: would maybe stay over winter, or, being and working out of

speaker: JBM
words: Bovill, might go out just on jobs out of town, but they

speaker: JBM
words: board in town. Whereas the Spokane Hotel had an awful lot

speaker: JBM
words: more overnighters that were just, you know, in town for a

speaker: JBM
words: night or two and headed out. But then there were old people

speaker: JBM
words: like Pat Malone, for example, who boarded all full-time at

speaker: JBM
words: the Spokane hotel. Yes, there were places here where lum

speaker: JBM
words: berjacks stayed for fairly long terms, but many of them did

speaker: JBM
words: go out when they were over winter.

speaker: JBM
words: But the camps ran during the winter a lot, perhaps

speaker: JBM
words: more than now. Because you have to consider that theseheavy

speaker: JBM
words: machines that the/re using today--rubber tired log

speaker: JBM
words: haulers or even a tractor—you get it out and work in deep

speaker: JBM
words: snow and in mud and so forth, and trucks particularly, and

speaker: JBM
words: they will mire down. Well, in the old days everything was

speaker: JBM
words: on rails, and the mud didn't bother the teams so much. As

speaker: JBM
words: a matter of fact, so far as the teams were concerned, the

speaker: JBM
words: mud up to a skidding log is a good lubricant, and snow is

speaker: JBM
words: also a good lubricant. In a heavy snow they could use sleighs,

speaker: JBM
words: and haul logs on sleighs, or they can skid through the snow,

speaker: JBM
words: much better than they can get out in deep snow with this

speaker: JBM
words: equipment. So they ran in the winter a great deal;,

speaker: SAM
words: There's one guy that I've talked to, said he quit log

speaker: SAM
words: ging when they went to the more seasonal logging, when they

speaker: SAM
words: started getting the heavier equipment, because he couldn't

speaker: SAM
words: work as long as he used to be able to before... What about

speaker: SAM
words: the difference in morals between the lumberjack and the

speaker: SAM
words: family man? Was that a problem in the community, or how

speaker: SAM
words: was it dealt with?

speaker: JBM
words: I can't remember that anything like that was a problem,

speaker: JBM
words: excepting of course in those days drinking was something of

speaker: JBM
words: a problem. Drinking was, in the years of prohibition, was

speaker: JBM
words: against the law, and possession of liquor was against the

speaker: JBM
words: law, so there was always a conflict between the drinking

speaker: JBM
words: lumberjack and the law from that standpoint. I don't,—

speaker: JBM
words: I was probably too young to appreciate altogether the aspects

speaker: JBM
words: of the moral life of the lumberjack otherwise, but I suspect

speaker: JBM
words: for his women he generally went to Spokane. There were

speaker: JBM
words: plenty of them available up there, and in a small town they

speaker: JBM
words: just aren't very available.

speaker: SAM
words: You do talk about huses though, at least for a period

speaker: SAM
words: of time in the early days.

speaker: JBM
words: That was in the saloon days, and of course it was

speaker: JBM
words: legal at that time. So far as I know, the open prostitution

speaker: JBM
words: disappeared when the saloons went out of business, because

speaker: JBM
words: they were kind of tied together, I think, the serving liquor

speaker: JBM
words: and the prostitution. I don't suppose there were any town

speaker: JBM
words: hardly but what you will find a small percentage of immoral

speaker: JBM
words: women. It was much more underground, anyway, after that,

speaker: JBM
words: whatever did happen. I don't actually know. I so know

speaker: JBM
words: about the bawdy houses, but just for a time.

speaker: SAM
words: The picture you paint of Pat Malone has really been borne

speaker: SAM
words: out by people that I've talked to who agree, in fact, have

speaker: SAM
words: given me other stories about Pat's ideas about havigi-a

speaker: SAM
words: good time and drinking himself, which makes it seem that

speaker: SAM
words: prohibition was sort of a farce out here.

speaker: JBM
words: Well, it certainly was. Pat Malone was probably the most,

speaker: JBM
words: in many senses, the most colorful guy that ever hit the town.

speaker: JBM
words: (jaufihs), He was a character, and there'll never be another

speaker: JBM
words: like him, probably. It was just that sort of a situation.

speaker: SAM
words: I've been trying to figure out who he ever did arrest,

speaker: SAM
words: 'cause everybody that I've talked to seems to have been a

speaker: SAM
words: good friend and went out drinking with him and...

speaker: JBM
words: I think he preferred it that way. When he made an arrest,

speaker: JBM
words: why I think somebody was pushing him. He'd better get busy.

speaker: JBM
words: Yes, you'll find the town is full of stories about Pat

speaker: JBM
words: Malone, and you probably could never come to the end of them.

speaker: JBM
words: I mean, if you could have gotten into town twenty

speaker: JBM
words: years ago you'd have heard twenty times as many, because

speaker: JBM
words: there were twenty-nine times as many people that knew old

speaker: JBM
words: Pat as now exist. He was just the character that people

speaker: JBM
words: most liked to laugh about, that was all, but it didn't

speaker: JBM
words: mean at all that they didn't like him. I think his actual

speaker: JBM
words: value to the community was largely ignored, because it

speaker: JBM
words: didn't fit the image that people liked to have of him, and

speaker: JBM
words: it really didn't... Since he was a kind of a laughable

speaker: JBM
words: character, of course, he was going toi have that image. I

speaker: JBM
words: don't imagine that people would have had nearly so much to

speaker: JBM
words: laugh about, about old Pat, if he hadn't been in the law

speaker: JBM
words: enforcement side of things, and obliged to at least make token

speaker: JBM
words: manifestations of ding something with regard to liquor.

speaker: JBM
words: And, of course, since people were going to drink anyway,

speaker: JBM
words: why, they just made as much fun out of it as they: could.

speaker: JBM
words: (Chuckle) He was part of the fun.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, what do you think then that his value was?

speaker: JBM
words: Well, in my mind there's little doubt that the presence

speaker: JBM
words: of a man in an official category... If there really is a

speaker: JBM
words: situation develops where authority is needed, the presence

speaker: JBM
words: of such a man does stabilize a community. If anything ever

speaker: JBM
words: does happen that the law needs teeth, why people will back

speaker: JBM
words: up the man who has the authority. And knowing that this

speaker: JBM
words: is so, why, it keeps things "in hand.

speaker: ROB
words: Do you recall any situations like that?

speaker: JBM
words: Oh, I recall times when Pat made arrests. I do know

speaker: JBM
words: also that Pat, to a certain extent, he helped the town keep

speaker: JBM
words: control of such laws as, say the herd law, where it has to

speaker: JBM
words: keep the cows out of town. They had a town pound where

speaker: JBM
words: they would pick up cows if they were wandering in the streets,

speaker: JBM
words: which was illegal, and put them in the pound and there would

speaker: JBM
words: be a fine. He enforced that sort of thing. I told in my

speaker: JBM
words: book about one arrest he made out here at Camp 7. There

speaker: JBM
words: were other arrests that he made, I know, where the offenses

speaker: JBM
words: were less severe, minor thefts and things like that. I

speaker: JBM
words: justj I don't recollect names at the time, but I'm aware

speaker: JBM
words: that things like that happened from time to time.

speaker: JBM
words: There was the instance out here where they had the

speaker: JBM
words: fight in the cedar yard. The two men fought and one of them

speaker: JBM
words: was killed. Well, an arrest is needed. The presence of

speaker: JBM
words: the deputy sheriff makes it possible. But I think more

speaker: JBM
words: than anything in the very early days, when the saloons were

speaker: JBM
words: here, there was more rough and tumble fighting where drunk

speaker: JBM
words: enness is common in the streets. There are many drunks

speaker: JBM
words: in the streets. After prohibition, the drunks generally

speaker: JBM
words: try to become inconspicuous. They try to stay off the

speaker: JBM
words: streets. But when there's no prohibition, they're out

speaker: JBM
words: in the street, and when you get a bunch of drunks together

speaker: JBM
words: you're gonna have trouble. Pat would haul them away to

speaker: JBM
words: jail, if it got too bad. And he wasn't a firm man, he

speaker: JBM
words: wasn't a man who would just, yau know, dominate the people.

speaker: JBM
words: But still, by and large, the people are law abiding people,

speaker: JBM
words: and if he needed help, why these men were his friends, bas

speaker: JBM
words: ically. And if one man got out of hand and Pat needed to

speaker: JBM
words: haul him away, why there was somebody to help.

speaker: ROB
words: Basically what you're saying then is he interpreted the

speaker: ROB
words: law himself. He interpreted things that were neccessay to

speaker: ROB
words: have a legal..

speaker: JBM
words: I suspect if he had been a club-swinging cop or a guntoting

speaker: JBM
words: cop that tried to handle the law in a hard-fisted

speaker: JBM
words: way, he might have run into more trouble than he did just

speaker: JBM
words: persuading people, you know.

speaker: JBM
words: ( End of Side A)

speaker: JBM
words: I've heard young people mention that they were afraid

speaker: JBM
words: of Pat. It never occurred to me to be afraid of Pat. But

speaker: JBM
words: young people, just the fact that he was a policeman, he had

speaker: JBM
words: authority and that was enough, you know. There's very little

speaker: JBM
words: doubt that fear of the law is probably at least part of re

speaker: JBM
words: spect for the law, at least for some people. They probably

speaker: JBM
words: go hand in hand, to some extent. But he was such a mildmannered

speaker: JBM
words: fellow that it never occurred to me to be afraid

speaker: JBM
words: of him, because it didn't occur to me to be doing the type

speaker: JBM
words: of thing that I had to fear him.

speaker: JBM
words: But I did hear one instance... I don't recall the in

speaker: JBM
words: dividuals, I know Davy Ellison was one of them that was

speaker: JBM
words: mentioned, that was involved in this. One time when some

speaker: JBM
words: of the kids got fairly noisy at some affair, and somebody

speaker: JBM
words: tried to quiet them down with very little effect, and Pat,

speaker: JBM
words: ususally, you know, he generally didn't take a hand in such

speaker: JBM
words: things; but in this case he did step up, and he just stepped

speaker: JBM
words: in among the boys and said, "Weil, quiet down," and they just

speaker: JBM
words: shut up right there. The law had spoken, so that was it.

speaker: JBM
words: There's a psychology there I think, from that standpoint,

speaker: JBM
words: I think he served the town's needs to a large degree.

speaker: ROB
words: It sounds like he really understood, riot intellectualizing

speaker: ROB
words: anything, just understood the town's needs and responded

speaker: ROB
words: to them.

speaker: JBM
words: I doubt if he did. I just think he was a kind of an

speaker: JBM
words: easy-going affable man, and he hated to... I don't think

speaker: JBM
words: he understood hardness or authority. I don't think he was

speaker: JBM
words: a tough man at all. But he was a man whose habit, his way

speaker: JBM
words: of dress, his way of talk, his way of living, was very much

speaker: JBM
words: like the lumberjack's way. I suspect that the fact that

speaker: JBM
words: he was affable made him much better liked among the lumber

speaker: JBM
words: jacks than if he had tried to be hard-handed. They didn't

speaker: JBM
words: pay much attention to him most of the time. They know they

speaker: JBM
words: didn't have to. They could kind of do what they liked to

speaker: JBM
words: do. If they got too far out of line, and he spoke up...

speaker: JBM
words: Why, if somebody was too drunk to listen or something, why

speaker: JBM
words: Pat, just because he was authority and he was kind of the

speaker: JBM
words: natural leader, at that point, the friend would calm down

speaker: JBM
words: the rowdy one. This is the way I picture it. And I think it

speaker: JBM
words: probably worked; in that type of people it probably worked

speaker: JBM
words: better than if he'd actually tried to just, you know, handle

speaker: JBM
words: a guy.

speaker: ROB
words: He tried to hold himself apart from...

speaker: JBM
words: Right.

speaker: SAM
words: You seem to have a special feeling for Pete Olson in the

speaker: SAM
words: book, the way you describe him. Was that a feeling that a

speaker: SAM
words: lot people in the community had about Pete?

speaker: JBM
words: Yes, I think Pete was another one of the people that

speaker: JBM
words: you could call a real character. People looked at him as

speaker: JBM
words: a character. And off by himself in many ways. He was com

speaker: JBM
words: ical in many ways. He was certainly a good citizen. He

speaker: JBM
words: certainly had a wonderful mind, there's no doubt about it.

speaker: JBM
words: People realized that he could remember things, and be more

speaker: JBM
words: accurate in many ways in his memory, and was better informed

speaker: JBM
words: by far, than a lot of people with possibly much more formal

speaker: JBM
words: education than Pete had. I don't really know what Pete's

speaker: JBM
words: education, formal education, was)but he was not short suit

speaker: JBM
words: ed. He certainly must have been up someplace in the high

speaker: JBM
words: school grade, or something like that. Beyond that, he had

speaker: JBM
words: a mind that acquired information. He had a curious mind,

speaker: JBM
words: and he had a very retentive memory, and he was an ardent

speaker: JBM
words: reader of anything and everything, almost. He

speaker: JBM
words: admired knowledge, but he had very quaint ways.

speaker: JBM

speaker: JBM
words: his farm. (The slaughter house set up in the middle of it,

speaker: JBM
words: the old slaughterhouse set up in the middle of it. That's

speaker: JBM
words: where they butchered the meat in the older days. Pete

speaker: JBM
words: acquired it somewhat later than that. In that period he

speaker: JBM
words: was just a logger, but he acquired that place and it be

speaker: JBM
words: came his farm.) And you'd see old Pete up there twelve

speaker: JBM
words: o'clock at night making hay by moonlight, and certainly

speaker: JBM
words: this isn't...(laugh).

speaker: ROB
words: That goes counter to the old saying, "Make hay while the

speaker: ROB
words: sun shines."

speaker: JBM
words: Well, he made hay all night long if the dew didn't

speaker: JBM
words: get too heavy. He never fit the conventional pattern

speaker: JBM
words: hardly in anything. I don't know how straight he came

speaker: JBM
words: here from Sweden. He did come into the community from Swe

speaker: JBM
words: den, I think probably around 1910. I never knew the exact

speaker: JBM
words: date.

speaker: JBM
words: (incidentally, this is one of the things that I found

speaker: JBM
words: out about from the Moore family. I had thought the Hayes

words: family came in here about 1909, and I found out from Mrs.

words: Moore's record down in Potlatch yesterday they were here

speaker: JBM
words: and became a very good citizen. He was a volunteer into

speaker: SAM
words: Do you think that there was a general attitude towards

speaker: SAM
words: Potlatch Forests in the community? Was it very individual,

speaker: SAM
words: or.

speaker: JBM
words: I think anything like that is individual. There was a

speaker: JBM
words: lot of antagonism toward the company, of course. I think

speaker: JBM
words: anytime you have a company or a corporation who is big, and

speaker: JBM
words: regarded as rich, and that has policies that are tough on

speaker: JBM
words: the workmen, that there is going to be some antagonism.

speaker: JBM
words: People continue to work for them. A great many of them admired

speaker: JBM
words: the bosses, and what-not, that they worked under. Still,

speaker: JBM
words: there is awareness tnat the company has got lots of money,

speaker: JBM
words: and they're making good money, and they're carrying on with

speaker: JBM
words: policies that the workman feels are not to his best interest.

speaker: JBM
words: The fact that there's been worse in the past, it leaves the

speaker: JBM
words: employee with a feeling that he only makes progress when he

speaker: JBM
words: demands better things, and that the company isn't really

speaker: JBM
words: ready to give him anything. There has to be some truth to this.

speaker: JBM
words: I doubt—in the struggle of the workingman against big

speaker: JBM
words: business, I doubt if the workingman would have ever made,

speaker: JBM
words: gotten, very far if he hadn't struggled, if he hadn't union

words: ized, and so forth. And yet today I'm not very much a

speaker: JBM

words: among the loggers here, and they were hated by many of the

speaker: JBM
words: people who regarded themselves to be the more law-and-order

speaker: JBM
words: type. I know my dad... Almost the worst thing he could call

speaker: JBM
words: a man would be an IWW, and he felt that was kind, hunh, of

speaker: JBM
words: bottom of the well. I guess my—the Olson family in Deary,

speaker: JBM
words: my uncle and aunt, they must have had about the sam# opinion.

speaker: JBM
words: when I had this manuscript ready, and she was reading about

words: the IWW's. And her father, Emery Olson, had been a. clerk

words: over in Moscow to make arrests and to conduct the suspects

words: with somebody he'd picked up, that had been an accused IWW

words: member, and accused of something or otiher. And he'd pick

words: him up in camp and bring him through, and perhaps stop at

words: home for a few minutes, and maybe give him a meal or something.

words: But the fellow would sit down in one of the family chairs

words: someplace, and afterwards the girls were almost afraid to

speaker: JBM
words: sit in the chair because it had been contaminated by an IWW.

speaker: JBM
words: discrepent opinions about... first about the worth of IWW,

words: second about what acts they did. For example, I tell about

words: this dropping a boom across a pole pile in the cedar yard

words: here, by a man who, well, he'd been newly hired. It was

words: his first day on the job and they'd put him on to operate

words: and you hurt 'em whenever you can.

words: I mean, maybe they're not IWW's, because you'd very seldom

words: know whether they actually carry a card. These cards were

speaker: JBM
words: carried secretly. You know that they are sympathizers. But

speaker: JBM
words: you knew these people a long time, and you know that they're

speaker: JBM
words: honest people, they're hard working people, and they're good

speaker: JBM
words: friends, they're good neighbors. Your're not going to think

speaker: JBM
words: very bad of them in the long run. You wish they weren't

speaker: JBM
words: sympathetic to the IWW, it kind of amounts to that. But on

speaker: JBM
words: the other hand, a tough lumberjack who's a stranger to you,

speaker: JBM
words: and you hear him expounding IWW talk, and if you're against

speaker: JBM
words: it, why them you right wvay classify him, he's one of these

speaker: JBM
words: so-and-so's, you see. He can't be trusted. So it's a

speaker: JBM
words: matter of degree, and it's a matter of who he is and how

speaker: JBM
words: well you know him, to a certain extent.

speaker: ROB
words: You said about the burning of the grain fields. If the

speaker: ROB
words: IWW was primarily in this area in a dispute with Potlatch,

speaker: ROB
words: Thy would they want to carry the dispute over, or why would

speaker: ROB
words: they want to antagonize the farmers?

speaker: JBM
words: The IWW was deeper than that. You have to remember

speaker: JBM
words: that the IWW didn't even start in the woods. It was present

speaker: JBM
words: up in the mines much before it was among the lumberjacks.

speaker: JBM
words: It was something of a country-wide movement,and it was a

speaker: JBM
words: communist led thing, originally. And again, I don't think

speaker: JBM
words: that every IWW sympathizer, particularly, certainly not in

speaker: JBM
words: an area like Bovill, where you're dealing with labor matters,

speaker: JBM
words: labor disputes... They don't look deeply into it to know

speaker: JBM
words: whether it's communist or not communist, or care very much,

speaker: JBM
words: if it serves their purpose, immediate purpose. But the

speaker: JBM
words: communist doctrine actually is aimed at undermining the

speaker: JBM
words: government, so that anything they could do, if you got among

speaker: JBM
words: the real hard core communists among them, anything that they

speaker: JBM
words: could do to create disorder, to hurt the government in any

speaker: JBM
words: kind or a backhanded way, they were ready to do.

speaker: JBM
words: Now the average lumberjack who took part in this movement,

speaker: JBM
words: he was more concerned with something else. He was living

speaker: JBM
words: out in a lumbercamp that was very inadequate for his needs.

speaker: JBM
words: He was infested with bedbugs and lice and perhaps other

speaker: JBM
words: things, vermin, that he didn't much like. He didn't think

speaker: JBM
words: he ought to put up with them. Ke didn't like the fact that

speaker: JBM
words: there were not clean beds in the camp, and laundry facilities.

speaker: JBM
words: If he wanted to wash his clothes he had to go down, get an

speaker: JBM
words: iron bucket of some kind and go down and bust a hole in the

speaker: JBM
words: ice at the creek, and dip out water and get firewood, build

speaker: JBM
words: a fire and heat the water over the fire, and launder his clothes.

speaker: JBM
words: And there was no place to dry them after he got 'em laundered,

speaker: JBM
words: and you try to y clotiies in the middle of the winter without

speaker: JBM
words: facilities. And he came in dirty and wet maybe after a

speaker: JBM
words: stormy, rainy day, and his clothes were wet with rain end

speaker: JBM
words: sweated full and so forth. Wool smells bad when it's wet

speaker: JBM
words: if it's clean, and when it's dirty, it smells ten times as

speaker: JBM
words: bad. And here he is in a little narrow bunk house, a bunk

speaker: JBM
words: shack with tiers of bunks on both sides of him, and up the

speaker: JBM
words: center of aisle they stretch a line, and ail the men hang

speaker: JBM
words: their clothes on the line, and they're about that far from

speaker: JBM
words: your face, and you're trying to sleep. Those things are

speaker: JBM
words: stinking to high heaven. And there was nothing being done

speaker: JBM
words: at all to clean up the beds, or to provide clean bedding

speaker: JBM
words: or laundry facilities in camp, or anything else. And the

speaker: JBM
words: company wasn't concerned with it. And he's in a conflict

speaker: JBM
words: with the company. Well, the IWW is a means of getting back

speaker: JBM
words: at the head of things and getting this thing put to rights.

speaker: JBM
words: It went a long way in accomplishing that.

words: told there was a strike, and do you know about that? And

words: I've also been told the strike resulted in the change of

speaker: SAM
words: conditions.

words: the mills being stopped and things when I was young, and I'm

words: not aware where these things happened. And I questioned some

words: of the locals here, and they tell me that work was never

words: stopped in the Bovill area due to strikes. I'm not in a

words: position to dispute it. I know in some parts of the North

words: west there were some fairly serious stopages. There was

speaker: JBM
words: also sabotage in the mills and things like hat. Equipment

words: was broken. I do know that the Potlatch Company was very

words: worried that they were going to suffer some severe loses due

speaker: JBM
words: to, not only to strikes but to... They were very much afraid

speaker: JBM
words: of fires and damage of equipment and that sort of thing.

speaker: JBM
words: But I don't know of instances where there was very much done,

speaker: JBM
words: nothing serious in the woods around Bovill. I don't know

speaker: JBM
words: whether the Potlatch mill ever might have been shut down,

words: but if it was, it was temporary. And it didn't stop, appar

speaker: JBM
words: ently it didn't stop any of the logging operations. I don't

words: think the Elk River mill was ever shut down by strikes. But

words: I have not investigated that point thoroughly, and I have

speaker: JBM
words: run into people who swear that it didn't happen. I think

words: you might have to... To investigate it thoroughly, you might

words: have to get newspapers and other sources.

words: Now, do you know if that's true and if it is, how that's come

speaker: SAM
words: It was about election returns that someone said to

speaker: SAM
words: me it goes strong Democratically.

words: blue shirt communities, where you have a wage-oriented

speaker: JBM
words: people, do tend to go in the present day Democratic. In

words: the days of my boyhood 1 think Bovill went mostly Republican.

words: There was a strong swing toward the Democratic, as these was

speaker: JBM
words: nationwide, in Roosevelt's time. I don't think since that

words: time that you can... I think there are relatively few com

words: munities where the livelihood ; is largely based on physical

speaker: JBM
words: work, or blue collar type, blue shirt type work... That's

speaker: JBM
words: a poor term for lumberjacks because they use plaid shirts

speaker: JBM
words: (laugh) I guess, but...

speaker: SAM
words: Plaid shirt work.

speaker: JBM
words: I think when you have that kind of a wage base, ever

speaker: JBM
words: since the Depression years there has been a tendency for a

speaker: JBM
words: Democratic vote. And of course today the Democrats nation

speaker: JBM
words: wide outnumber Republicans almost two to one, I guess, and

words: the only salvaging thin is there's an intervening group;

words: I mean the non-affiliated voters help to balance things out

words: a little bit more, But I would guess they probably do, and

words: I would guess it dates back to the Depression years. But

words: know the sentiments very well.

speaker: JBM
words: SAl'I: What do you think has happened to Bovill in recent years,

speaker: JBM
words: since you've left and you come back and see what it's like

speaker: JBM
words: now? And does it give you any ideas about where it might

speaker: JBM
words: be headed?

speaker: JBM
words: Well, the area in general, so far as natural beauty is

speaker: JBM
words: concerned, I think is coming up, or has been coming up, in

speaker: JBM
words: the last forty years. I think about the low point might

speaker: JBM
words: have been about 1930 when the very hard logging of the prime

speaker: JBM
words: timber in the near vicinity of Bovill was finished. And

speaker: JBM
words: of course the logging operations leave jst almost an un

speaker: JBM
words: believable litter and mess behind. And particularly that

speaker: JBM
words: type of logging did leave the woods badly littered, with

speaker: JBM
words: the broken trees and onburned litter of all types. Of

speaker: JBM
words: course that had been obliterated by time to a large extent,

speaker: JBM
words: and you've got the new growth. And it's getting large enough

speaker: JBM
words: now to restore an awful lot of natural beauty. In the mean

speaker: JBM
words: time, though, the town has run down just unbelievably. Bovill,

speaker: JBM
words: I think, has... perhaps of the small towns in this near

speaker: JBM
words: vicinity, it has the prettiest natural setting of any town,

speaker: JBM
words: and it has kind of a nice layout to the town. You come

speaker: JBM
words: across the hill from,.. Beginning at the time you cross the

speaker: JBM
words: hill and look at the town, and the way it lays down here

speaker: JBM
words: against the mountains,'and the background and so forth, in

speaker: JBM
words: among the trees and so forth, it has a nice look. 3ut now

speaker: JBM
words: adays, when you get down in the middle of the town and see

speaker: JBM
words: how shoddy everything is, and so many of the houses have

speaker: JBM
words: been let run down; and worse than all, you go down and see

speaker: JBM
words: what happened to Main Street, the lower part of Main Street,

speaker: JBM
words: it's just an absolute wreck. This is very depressing.

speaker: JBM
words: Now as to the future. I would suppose that the future

speaker: JBM
words: may not look... At times the beauty may be marred by logging

speaker: JBM
words: of the young timber, because the young timber is getting

speaker: JBM
words: large enough so they're beginning to cut it. I feel they

speaker: JBM
words: are cutting it prematurely. Evidently the timber is needed

speaker: JBM
words: that badly, and it is cheap enough to get the nearby small

speaker: JBM
words: stuff out so that it can be done. And unfortunately, in

speaker: JBM
words: our type of political situations, ah, set up... You can say

speaker: JBM
words: this against that capital, capitalism: it's all profitoriented,

speaker: JBM
words: and that means getting goods form the producer to

speaker: JBM
words: the consumer as cheaply as possible. So even though it may

speaker: JBM
words: not be good for the long-term benefit of the public to do

speaker: JBM
words: something now... It might be much better to wait another

speaker: JBM
words: twenty years to cut this stuff. And here they're cutting

speaker: JBM
words: stuff now that's perhaps twelve to sixteen inches, and if

speaker: JBM
words: they waited another twenty years, why they'd be cutting stuff

speaker: JBM
words: that might be fifteen to twenty inches in diameter, you see.

speaker: JBM
words: And this would make much better timber. And in the meantime

speaker: JBM
words: there is, back towards the headwaters of the Clearwater,

speaker: JBM
words: other areas, I understand... there is still matured timber

speaker: JBM
words: that is being... it's joust over—mature and the bugs get in

speaker: JBM
words: it and are ruining it. That stuff ought to be taken out,

speaker: JBM
words: ought to be taken out now before it goes bad, and this stuff

speaker: JBM
words: allowed to grow.

speaker: SAM
words: Do youthir that there is a general feeling in the oldtimers

speaker: SAM
words: about how the forests have been managed?

speaker: JBM
words: It seems to me that everybody must be aware that there

speaker: JBM
words: is some mismanagement. I don't actually know what is the

speaker: JBM
words: best way to handle forests. The present trend of course

speaker: JBM
words: has been, at least in some areas, they've gone on this strip

speaker: JBM
words: logging type thing, you know; and they just knock down every33

speaker: JBM
words: thing and just leave nothing standing. I'm not an expert

speaker: JBM
words: enough-in this kind of field to weigh one thing against

speaker: JBM
words: another. In the old days T. P. Jones' policy, for example,

speaker: JBM
words: was to just set the brush and slash on fire and let it burn,

speaker: JBM
words: and if it burned some small stuff, why fine, but usually

speaker: JBM
words: some small stuff survived. And if you go .back over those

speaker: JBM
words: areas today, most of those areas have grown up to a pretty

speaker: JBM
words: nice stand of timber. Maybe there's a lot to be said for

speaker: JBM
words: doing it that way. It was certainly... At the time it was

speaker: JBM
words: done for expediency. This was the cheapest way to get the

speaker: JBM
words: timber out and to leave the ground, you now, to leave the

speaker: JBM
words: area and get r,id of his mess to a certain extent. But these

speaker: JBM
words: areas are grown up to nice stands of fine timber. And just

speaker: JBM
words: leveling everything to the ground may not be the best thing

speaker: JBM
words: to do. I think foresters are going to have to take, should

speaker: JBM
words: take a long, hard look at what they're doing. But more than

speaker: JBM
words: anything, they shouldni't be letting forests go to waste in

speaker: JBM
words: one area just because it's cheaper to cut logs in another;

speaker: JBM
words: and this is the type of titing that's done too much.

speaker: JBM
words: They shouldn't... Well, you get the same thing in mining

speaker: JBM
words: now, for example. People go underground after some ore.

speaker: JBM
words: You've got some low grade areas and high grade areas, and it

speaker: JBM
words: really pays to mine the high grade. So you bypass the low

speaker: JBM
words: grade and then you perhaps never can afford to go back after

speaker: JBM
words: it, you see. You're going to leave metal in the ground that

speaker: JBM
words: is going to be badly needed in the future. Or, certain

speaker: JBM
words: parts of the body run to zinc and others to copper, and there's

speaker: JBM
words: a good sale for copper so you leave the zinc behind. And

speaker: JBM
words: this is not economical on the long term, because all of

speaker: JBM
words: these metals are going to be scarce someday. So, as I say,

speaker: JBM
words: the same thing... We're on this, now so much interested in

speaker: JBM
words: the environment. But our economic system is set up to help,

speaker: JBM
words: to buy the product from the man who can sell it the cheapest.

speaker: JBM
words: And if he goes out and ruins the land to get the product to

speaker: JBM
words: market two dollars a ton cheaper, why, his product is bought.

speaker: JBM
words: This is the way our system works, and it's not right. It's

speaker: JBM
words: a short term thing.

speaker: SAM
words: How would that change? Is it an ethic that people have to

speaker: SAM
words: have, or is it government that would have to change the situation?

speaker: JBM
words: The public viewpoint on this is changing, but I'm not

speaker: JBM
words: sure it's changing inan educated way. I wonder if people

speaker: JBM
words: understand. They want to do things better, but I do feel

speaker: JBM
words: that, probably, in the background of the average individual,

speaker: JBM
words: there's not enough knowledge about the economics of things,

speaker: JBM
words: about resources and their nature, our ability to replace

speaker: JBM
words: them. There are too many people with the idea, for example,

speaker: JBM
words: that the earth's resources, oil and metal and things like

speaker: JBM
words: that, is ILmitless, and that as you use up what you've got

speaker: JBM
words: now, you'll find new ways to get more. And this only has

speaker: JBM
words: at best a degree of truth in it, but it's certainly not

speaker: JBM
words: entirely true. Too many of these things, once thsy're gone,

speaker: JBM
words: they're gone forver, unlike the forest, which can be grown

speaker: JBM
words: again.

speaker: JBM
words: There is a breakover point where it is no longer

speaker: JBM
words: feasible to go in and mine low grade ore, for example. For

speaker: JBM
words: one thing, the lower grade the ore, the more energy it's

speaker: JBM
words: going to take to process it into metal. The lower grade

speaker: JBM
words: the ore, the bigger machine is to get it out, to move it

speaker: JBM
words: economically. You get to a breakover point where you can't

speaker: JBM
words: afford to operate it anymore for the metal you extract, why

speaker: JBM
words: it's not gonna work.

speaker: ROB
words: You also use more energy to get it than you get back from

speaker: ROB
words: it.

speaker: JBM
words: And you're using up energy that you can't afford, in

speaker: JBM
words: a situation in which the energy supply is diminishing. Can

speaker: JBM
words: you replace the inner resource? Nobod} knows yet. Can you

speaker: JBM
words: replace it with something new? And T 'on't think anybody

speaker: JBM
words: knows for sure. With regard to oil, even if you could re

speaker: JBM
words: place the energy, what do you do for lubricants, you see?

speaker: JBM
words: You run into that kind of a problem. You can't help but

speaker: JBM
words: use your raw materials, but you ought to use them with an

speaker: JBM
words: eye to the future. You shouldn't be using them just with

speaker: JBM
words: the degree of "how do I do something the cheapest and easiest

speaker: JBM
words: today, and the heck with the future." It shouldn't be done

speaker: JBM
words: in that way, and yet that's that way we operate.

speaker: ROB
words: Well, it seems to be sort of a frontier mentality that is

speaker: ROB
words: now becoming... Feople are realizing it's really no longer

speaker: ROB
words: viable.

speaker: JBM
words: They're realizing it's no longer viable, but I don't

speaker: JBM
words: think many people realize the degree, or in some ways, even

speaker: JBM
words: the proximity of possible disaster in this area. You stop

speaker: JBM
words: to think that, in metals, for example, you know how much

speaker: JBM
words: was used up in the late war alone. It was tremendous. And

speaker: JBM
words: yet since the war, the world has used more metals than in

speaker: JBM
words: all the previous world's history, including the war. And

speaker: JBM
words: you are going up on that exponential curve all the time.

speaker: JBM
words: How long can this happen? How long can we exist on an ex

speaker: JBM
words: pansion economy? We think we have to expand the economy

speaker: JBM
words: by something like five percent per year in order to have

speaker: JBM
words: a viable economy. This is disastrous, you cn't do that.

speaker: JBM
words: There isn't a supply of material, raw materials or anything,

speaker: JBM
words: that can take care of the type of demand you'd have ten

speaker: JBM
words: years from now on this exponential curve. Mathematically

speaker: JBM
words: an impossibility, and if you can proceed in this way, the

speaker: JBM
words: end, the disaster, is just as inevitable as death, because

speaker: JBM
words: it's the only thing that can happen.

words: me of what you say, you know, at the end of the book. At

words: the very end you say, "Were it possible to go back, there's

words: little doubt. It would be a rush." And it does seem like

words: the a lot of oldtimers do remember the past as being better.

words: was more satisfaction in a way. Part of what I talked about,

speaker: JBM
words: I guess, part of what I had in mind, is the sentiments. I

words: example, antiques, reflects this mentality. There is a

words: to a lot of people. There are just all kinds of motives

words: that are behind that sort of thing. The old life appeals to

words: a lot of people even though they know that it was a rough

words: life. If they got back and had to live that kind of a life,

words: I'm not sure they would want to stay ther very long, but

speaker: JBM
words: still the idea appeals to them.

speaker: JBM
words: There was a contentment about it, there's no doubt about

speaker: JBM
words: it. The life was tranquil, fulfilling in many ways. I

speaker: JBM
words: think that the human being has... I've always kind of felt

words: that an individual has a kind of a capacity for happiness,

words: and if his aspirations are fairly well met, he's a happy

words: person. And of course people in those days did not aspire

speaker: JBM
words: to an awful lot they didn't have, any more than they do today.

speaker: JBM
words: People always want something they don't have and probably

speaker: JBM
words: the relatively quiet life that people enjoyed in...

speaker: JBM
words: (End of ide ft)

speaker: JBM
words: We were talking abbut the kind of life that people

speaker: JBM
words: enjoyed in those days. I was mentioning the fact that the

speaker: JBM
words: life was built around home life. You had the conveniences

words: that you were accustomed to. You didn't always have the same

words: that you aspired to, and I think the same thing is true to

words: day. And you may be less frustrated, because you were less

words: aware of better things elsewhere. You didn't get around to

words: see what other people had so much. You lived at home. Now,

speaker: JBM
words: in a town like Bovill, there wasn't really that wide a

speaker: JBM
words: difference between the way one family lived and another.

speaker: JBM
words: But people, as I say, people have a capacity to be happy,

speaker: JBM
words: and they were nappy under it. I can't today think that I

speaker: JBM
words: enjoy life at all more because I have, let's say, better

speaker: JBM
words: conveniences I had them, which was all I knew about and was

speaker: JBM
words: used to having. And there was an enchantment about the times,

speaker: JBM
words: and it's part of this whole sentiment that you have for the

speaker: JBM
words: past. It's kind of hard to describe, but I guess that's at

speaker: JBM
words: the bottom of it all.

speaker: JBM
words: People worked awfully hard. I do know that, for example,

speaker: JBM
words: my mother's life, her workday, would absolutely amaze women

words: today. Ijust can't imagine a woman today working the way

words: she worked. Iknow she got very tired of the long past, but

words: she never complained about it, and she was happy. When she

words: got old she was was very thankful that she didn't have to

words: schedule and would have been just as happy as ever, and

words: perhaps in many ways she might have been happier with the

words: old life than the new. As I think most people might, because

words: it interests them, it's the thing that, they kind of feel

words: that it's right. There are many things they would not like

words: to go back to, but they would probably face it and do just

speaker: JBM
words: like the people then did then, they would struggle to make

speaker: JBM
words: things better, you know.

speaker: JBM
words: For example, when I was young, I just give you an ex

speaker: JBM
words: ample of what kind of day my mother might have. She was

speaker: JBM
words: probably up at no later than five in the morning, build a

speaker: JBM
words: fire first thing. She and my dad would take the milk pails

speaker: JBM
words: out to the barn and milk the cows, and she would milk probably

speaker: SAM
words: Do you see any major turning points in Bovill's history?

speaker: JBM
words: The turning point as I see it in the town was the

speaker: JBM
words: Depression years, which more or less marked the time of the

speaker: JBM
words: exhaustion of the large stands of prime timber near the

speaker: JBM
words: town. After that they had to step way back into the head

speaker: JBM
words: waters, and lots of times over the divide into the Clear

speaker: JBM
words: water drainage and that sort of thing, for the extensive

speaker: JBM
words: amounts of timber. There were little patches of big timber

speaker: JBM
words: left, here and there, but nothing very substantial. The

speaker: JBM
words: Depression put a stop on the logging that had existed, and

speaker: JBM
words: it more or less coincided with the time of the exhaustion

speaker: JBM
words: of that kind of timber supplies. It went straight on in

speaker: JBM
words: through from there into the war, when timber was needed, but

speaker: JBM
words: there was a limited opportunity to log in a big way because

speaker: JBM
words: of the war situation. They were coming out of it during the

speaker: JBM
words: war. Logging did start to expand again, but it expanded in

speaker: JBM
words: a new pattern. They started a railroad out of town (the grade

speaker: JBM
words: runs up south of town there), and it was to go on out through

speaker: JBM
words: the East Fork and across into the Clearwater, and sometime,

speaker: JBM
words: I think that was actually during the early war years they

speaker: JBM
words: started that thing... And somehow or other they realized

speaker: JBM
words: that logging was going to turn around, and that rails was

speaker: JBM
words: not going to be any longer the way of moving logs. So they

speaker: JBM
words: backed up and built a crushed rock road, and this was really...

speaker: JBM
words: So you see it led, bridged from the end of the lush logging

speaker: JBM
words: of the twenties into the depression, and then into a new type

speaker: JBM
words: of logging that began about during the war and stopped back

speaker: JBM
words: much farther for the primary supplies of timber.

words: just after the war; that was rather a cricial thing for the

speaker: JBM
words: I have a feeling that all of the public throughout the land,

words: there has been a degeneration, in a way, of moral fiber.
